users.emptySubtitle: There are no users with votes yet in this cineforum
users.pageSubtitle: Behavioural analysis of votes and individual trends
users.loveReceivedSubtitle: How other users rated movies proposed by this user
users.loveReceivedDescription: How other users rated on average the movies voted by {{user}}. The table shows the average rating received from each user.
users.loveGivenDescription: How {{user}} rated on average the movies proposed by other users. Avg Given: the average rating {{user}} gave to movies proposed by the row's user. Avg Ranking: the row user's global average in the cineforum (for comparison).
users.ratingDistributionSubtitle: How many movies you rated with each score
users.voterProfileExplanation: This profile analyses the user's voting behaviour relative to the group. Tendency indicates whether the user tends to vote higher (generous) or lower (strict) than the global average. Consistency measures how variable the votes are: a consistent user gives similar scores, while a variable one alternates between very high and very low. Consensus agreement shows how often the user votes above or below the movie average.
users.aboveConsensusTooltip: The percentage of times the user voted above the movie average. Indicates how often the user is more generous than the general consensus on each movie.
users.avgDeviationTooltip: The average of the absolute differences between the user's votes and each movie's average. Measures how much the user typically deviates from the consensus, regardless of direction (above or below). Higher values indicate more divergent opinions from the group.
users.userAverageTooltip: The average of all votes cast by this user in the cineforum. Indicates the user's general tendency to vote high or low.
users.globalAverageTooltip: The average of all votes cast by all users in the cineforum. Represents the "general consensus" of the group.
users.deltaTooltip: The difference between the user's average and the global average. Positive: the user votes higher than average (generous). Negative: the user votes lower than average (strict).
users.similarUsersSubtitle: Calculated on commonly rated movies, combining taste correlation, rating closeness, and the reliability of the number of shared movies (min. 15 movies).
users.similarUsersEmpty: No users with sufficiently similar taste found (min. 15 movies in common, Pearson > 0.25).
users.colCompatibilityTooltip: Final compatibility score expressed as a percentage. It is the product of Pearson × Vote proximity × Reliability weight. The higher it is, the more similar the tastes.
users.colPearsonTooltip: Pearson correlation between the two users' ratings on common movies. Measures whether you vote in the same direction relative to your personal averages. +1.0 = identical tastes, 0.0 = no correlation, -1.0 = opposite tastes. Minimum threshold: > 0.25.
users.colCommonMoviesTooltip: Number of movies rated by both users. The more movies in common, the more reliable the calculation. Minimum threshold: 15 movies. With 50+ movies the reliability weight is at its maximum (1.0).
users.colVoteProximityTooltip: How close the ratings are in absolute value, regardless of direction. Formula: 1 − (average absolute difference / 4). Average diff 0 → 100%, average diff 2 → 50%, average diff 4 → 0%. Two users can have a high Pearson but systematically distant ratings — this factor penalises those cases.
users.colUserTooltip: Name of the user with similar taste. Click the row to see the movie-by-movie comparison.
users.similarUsersAlgoIntro: The final score is the product of three factors, each capturing a different aspect of the similarity between two users:
users.algoFactor1Body: Measures whether two users tend to vote in the same direction relative to their own personal average. In other words: when you give a high rating, does the other user also tend to give a high rating? And when you give a low one, does he give a low one too?
users.algoFactor1WhyNotDiff: Why isn't the vote difference enough?
users.algoFactor1Example: Imagine Marco always votes 1 point above Giulia on every movie: Marco gives 4 where Giulia gives 3, 5 where Giulia gives 4, 2 where Giulia gives 1. The average difference is always 1, but the pattern is identical — they love the same movies and hate the same ones. The Pearson will be +1.0 because the votes move perfectly together, despite Marco's systematic bias.
users.algoFactor1Opposite: Conversely, if when Marco gives 5 Giulia gives 1 and vice versa, the Pearson will be -1.0 — opposite tastes. If there is no relationship between their votes, the Pearson will be close to 0.
users.algoFactor1Scale: +1.0 = identical tastes | 0.0 = no correlation | -1.0 = opposite tastes
users.algoFactor1Threshold: Minimum threshold: Pearson > 0.25. Below this threshold the user is excluded because the similarity is not reliable enough.
users.algoFactor1ZeroVariance: Special case: if a user rated all common movies with the same score (zero variance), the Pearson is mathematically undefined — it is treated as 0 and excluded.
users.algoFactor2Body: Pearson alone is not enough: two users can have a high correlation but systematically vote far apart in absolute value. This factor penalises pairs where votes, while moving in the same direction, are on average very distant.
users.algoFactor2Divisor: The divisor 4 is the maximum possible difference on a 1–5 scale (e.g. one gives 1, the other gives 5). So:
users.algoFactor2Diff0: Average difference 0.0 → penalty 1.0 (no penalty, identical votes)
users.algoFactor2Diff4: Average difference 4.0 → penalty 0.0 (votes always at opposite extremes)
users.algoFactor2Example: Marco and Giulia have Pearson = 0.90 (excellent correlation), but Marco always votes 2 points above Giulia. The average difference is 2.0, so the penalty is 0.50. This factor halves the final score — because even though they have similar tastes, their rating scales are very different.
users.algoFactor2ColNote: The "Vote proximity" column in the table shows this value as a percentage.
users.algoFactor3Body: A correlation calculated on few movies is less reliable than one calculated on many. With only 15 movies in common, it could be a coincidence. With 50+ movies, the similarity is statistically robust.
users.algoFactor3Threshold: Minimum common movies threshold: 15. Below this threshold the user is excluded entirely.
users.algoMinScore: Minimum final score threshold: 0.15. Users below this are excluded even if they pass the individual Pearson and common-movies thresholds.
users.algoClickHint: Click on a user in the table to see the movie-by-movie comparison with their respective ratings and the delta.
